name: Industrial 3D Printing
what: High-throughput additive production of end-use parts.

name: Construction 3D Printing
how: Concrete deposited along robotic path plans.

name: Bioprinting for Organ Regeneration
what: Advanced bioprinters capable of printing complex tissues and organs.
how: Utilizes living cells and biomaterials to create functional tissue structures.

name: Volumetric 3D Printing
what: Printing a whole object almost at once by solidifying resin throughout a volume rather than layer by layer.
how: Light patterns are projected from many angles into a rotating vat of photopolymer so the cumulative dose cures the target shape all together.

name: 4D Printing
what: Printing objects that later change shape on their own in response to heat, moisture or light.
how: Stimuli-responsive materials are printed with programmed internal stresses so the part folds or morphs into its final form after a trigger.

name: Bioprinting Vascularised Tissue
what: Printing living tissue with built-in blood-vessel channels, a key hurdle before thick lab-grown tissue can survive.
how: Cells and sacrificial inks are co-printed, then the sacrificial network is flushed to leave perfusable channels that feed the tissue.
use: Tissue models, drug testing, regenerative-medicine research

name: Metal Binder Jetting
what: A faster route to metal parts that prints binder onto metal powder and then sinters the shape solid.
how: Inkjet heads deposit binder layer by layer to form a fragile 'green' part, which is de-bound and sintered in a furnace into dense metal.

name: In-Space 3D Printing
what: Manufacturing parts and specialty materials in orbit, where microgravity can improve some products and resupply is costly.
how: Extrusion and other printers run aboard the space station to make tools on demand and produce materials such as fibre that benefit from microgravity.
